retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Develop proficiency in dosing and administering pegloticase to patients in the healthcare setting. Identify the potential adverse effects of pegloticase. Screen patients based on the contraindications against pegloticase therapy. Implement strategies with an interprofessional team for monitoring patients with gout and improving care coordination and communication when using pegloticase in adult patients with chronic gout refractory to conventional therapy to improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with unresponsive uric acid levels or who have 2 or more gout flares and subcutaneous tophi persisting despite treatment at maximum dosages of conventional therapy are considered refractory and qualify as indications for pegloticase. [2] Patients with increased uric acid levels and not experiencing gout symptoms are not recommended for treatment with pegloticase.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Pegloticase, an FDA-approved pegylated, recombinant uricase, helps manage chronic gout refractory to standard therapy among adult patients. Since its approval in 2010, this drug has demonstrated efficacy by catalyzing uric acid into the water-soluble purine metabolite allantoin, facilitating its easy excretion. This activity discusses pegloticase's indications, mechanisms of action, and contraindications, emphasizing its indispensable role in treating and managing chronic gout that remains unresponsive to conventional therapeutic approaches. Attendees will become more knowledgeable about pegloticase therapy, including its adverse event profile, enabling interprofessional team members involved in caring for patients with chronic gout and related conditions to make informed decisions within a clinical setting.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with pre-existing congestive heart failure planning to receive therapy with pegloticase should be carefully evaluated. Before initiating treatment, the potential benefits and risks should be assessed and discussed with the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic gout (chronic and refractory to conventional therapy)

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pegloticase is a pegylated, recombinant uricase (urate-oxidase) FDA-approved in 2010 for its use in managing chronic gout refractory to standard therapy in adult patients. [1] Conventional treatment for chronic gout includes xanthine oxidase inhibitors such as allopurinol and febuxostat. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Pegloticase is recommended to be co-administered with weekly oral methotrexate 15 mg alongside supplementation with folic acid or folinic acid. The use of pegloticase as monotherapy is not recommended, except in cases where using methotrexate is contraindicated or not suitable for the patient's clinical condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients receiving treatment should be counseled by their healthcare provider of no concomitant oral urate-lowering medications while on therapy with pegloticase. Patients with underlying CHF should be consulted with a cardiologist before initiation of treatment, as CHF exacerbation may occur while being treated. If pegloticase is considered in these patients, regular follow-ups are essential. The PCP and rheumatologist should routinely monitor uric acid levels as levels rising above 6 mg/dL or 2 sequential levels above 6 mg/dL are observed; suspending treatment should be considered.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients being treated should be thoroughly educated on chronic gout and treatment with pegloticase and its possible adverse effects. Alongside therapy, lifestyle modifications should also be counseled. The primary care provider and rheumatologist should maintain regular follow-ups to manage symptoms and maintain a normalized targeted range for uric acid levels. All interprofessional team members should also continually be updated with the latest guidelines on chronic gout management.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegfilgrastim -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Evaluate the mechanism of action of pegfilgrastim. Identify the indications for pegfilgrastim administration. Assess the adverse event profile of pegfilgrastim. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from pegfilgrastim therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Administration -- Available Dosage Forms and Strengths
retrieved_docs.content: The pegloticase diluted solution should be infused gradually over 120 minutes intravenously. Infusion should be delivered through gravity feed, syringe-type pump, or infusion pump. The recommended dosage of pegloticase for chronic refractory gout is 8 mg/mL (1 mL) administered intravenously (IV) every 2 weeks. Gout flare prophylaxis should start more than 1 week before initiating pegloticase therapy using NSAIDs or colchicine and should continue for at least 6 months.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegfilgrastim -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Pegfilgrastim may be administered to patients who are undergoing autologous transplantation. However, it should only be done under expert supervision. [7] According to the American Society for Blood and Marrow Transplantation, pegfilgrastim can be used with leukapheresis for chemotherapy plus growth factor mobilization. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegloticase -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Concomitant use of oral anti-hyperuricemic agents, including allopurinol, febuxostat, rasburicase, and lesinurad alongside pegloticase may result in are not recommended as they may result in delayed recognition of ineffective treatment and subsequently increase the risk of hypersensitivity and anaphylactoid like infusion reactions. Therefore, oral anti-hyperuricemic agents should be discontinued before initiating pegloticase therapy. Such agents also should not be started during treatment with pegloticase; this is essential to ensure an exact interpretation of serum uric acid levels in circulation and to minimize the risk of adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: Pegfilgrastim -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Pegfilgrastim can cause hypersensitivity reactions. [22] Therefore, it is contraindicated for patients with a history of sensitivity to pegfilgrastim, filgrastim, or any formulation component. The additional warnings and precautions as per labeling are given below.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone, danazol, desogestrel, and hydroxyprogesterone caproate are hormonal medications that are contraindicated in patients with liver disease (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد). The contraindication reflects the potential for hepatic toxicity or worsening of existing liver dysfunction associated with these agents. Consequently, clinicians should avoid prescribing any of these drugs to individuals with known liver disorders and should consider alternative therapeutic options to protect liver function and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1980
retrieved_docs.content: Colesevelam, a bile‑acid sequestrant used to lower cholesterol levels, is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis), indicating that this medication should not be prescribed to individuals suffering from this inflammatory pancreatic disease due to safety concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 266
retrieved_docs.content: topical use, ketoconazole is identified as having contraindications with several systemic agents. Dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are both contraindicated in the presence of ketoconazole, reflecting a class‑wide interaction risk. Conivaptan hydrochloride is similarly contraindicated with ketoconazole, and ketoconazole itself is contraindicated for use with ubrogepant. These contraindication relationships underscore the importance of reviewing a patient’s full medication profile before initiating ketoconazole‑containing therapies to avoid adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1452
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are a class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the drugs colestipol and colesevelam, both of which are classified as bile acid sequestrants. While colestipol can be used broadly within this class, colesevelam carries a specific contraindication: it should not be prescribed to patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس. Recognizing this disease‑specific restriction is essential for safe selection of a bile acid sequestrant in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: agents. Both dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate are classified as contraindicated with ketoconazole, as is conivaptan hydrochloride. Conversely, ketoconazole itself is listed as a contraindicated class for ubrogepant, meaning ubrogepant should be avoided in patients receiving ketoconazole. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse drug interactions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1228
retrieved_docs.content: Tetradecylsíran sodný, a sclerosing medication, is contraindicated in patients who have إنتان (sepsis), meaning it should not be administered when a systemic infection is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: with ketoconazole, indicating that co‑administration may precipitate adverse effects. Conivaptan hydrochloride is also contraindicated with ketoconazole, and ketoconazole itself is contraindicated with ubrogepant. These contraindications underscore the need for clinicians to review a patient’s medication regimen before prescribing ketoconazole‑containing products, particularly when the patient is receiving ergotamine derivatives, vasopressin antagonists, or CGRP receptor antagonists.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: Clindamycin is contraindicated in patients with hepatic failure (الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيّ), meaning the drug should not be administered to individuals suffering from liver failure due to the risk of worsening hepatic function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: It is classified as contraindicated with dihydroergotamine mesylate and dihydroergotamine tartrate, indicating that co‑administration of these ergotamine derivatives with ketoconazole‑containing products should be avoided. Similarly, conivaptan hydrochloride is listed as a contraindicated class of ketoconazole, reinforcing the need to prevent concurrent use. Conversely, ketoconazole is identified as a contraindicated class for ubrogepant, meaning that ubrogepant should not be used in patients receiving ketoconazole‑based therapies. Recognizing these contraindications is essential for safe prescribing and for minimizing adverse drug reactions in patients who require both antifungal topical treatment and other systemic medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1433
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants constitute a class of medications that includes colesevelam (also referred to as kolesevelam). Colesevelam functions as a bile acid sequestrant and is specifically contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس (pancreatitis). Consequently, clinicians should avoid prescribing coleselevam to individuals who have or are at risk for pancreatitis, recognizing that this contraindication is a key safety consideration for the use of this bile acid‑sequestering agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2216
retrieved_docs.content: Medium chain triglycerides are contraindicated in patients who have ارتفاع نسبة الدهون الثلاثية في الدم (elevated blood triglyceride levels), indicating that the use of medium chain triglycerides should be avoided in individuals with this condition because it may exacerbate or is not recommended for managing high triglyceride concentrations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1293
retrieved_docs.content: Bile acid sequestrants are classified as plain lipid‑modifying agents, a drug class used to lower cholesterol levels. Colesevelam is a specific medication that belongs to the bile acid sequestrant class. Because of its pharmacologic properties, colesevelam is contraindicated in patients who have الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس, and therefore should not be prescribed to individuals with this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 469
retrieved_docs.content: Hydroxyprogesterone caproate, nebivolol hydrochloride, fluvastatin, and nabumetone are all identified as medications that should be avoided in patients with liver disorder (اِضطرَاب الكَبِد). In addition, fluvastatin belongs to the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, which are plain lipid‑modifying drugs used to lower cholesterol. Other members of this class include atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin. Because fluvastatin is both a statin and contraindicated in liver disorder, clinicians should exercise caution when prescribing any of these agents to individuals with hepatic impairment, and consider alternative therapies for lipid management or other indications in such patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: Cyklosporin and nelfinavir mesylate are both identified as contraindicated when used together with simvastatin, indicating that co‑administration of either cyklosporin or nelfinavir mesylate with simvastatin should be avoided due to the risk of adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2659
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin and fluvastatin are members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the enzyme HMG‑CoA reductase, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, which leads to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As members of the same pharmacologic group, atorvastatin and fluvastatin share similar indications for treating hypercholesterolemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events, as well as comparable safety considerations such as the potential for myopathy and drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing both agents as part of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians apply class‑wide prescribing guidelines while selecting a specific statin based on potency, pharmacokinetic profile, and individual patient tolerance.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 685
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydroergotamin, erythromycin, simvastatin, cyklosporin, and nelfinavir mesylate are linked by several important drug‑drug contraindications. Dihydroergotamin should not be co‑administered with erythromycin because the two agents are contraindicated together. Erythromycin is also contraindicated with simvastatin, indicating that simultaneous use can increase the risk of adverse effects such as elevated statin concentrations. Simvastatin has additional contraindications with cyklosporin and with nelfinavir mesylate, reflecting a high potential for harmful interactions when these medications are combined. Recognizing and avoiding these contraindicated combinations is essential for safe prescribing and optimal patient care.
